coredata.dc:title: Dual-signal fluorescent test strips for spoilage sensing of packaged seafood: Visual monitoring of volatile basic nitrogens
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenyang
Ma, Ji
Sun, Da-Wen
coredata.dc:description: With the food safety issues abounding, exploring reliable and efficient methods for evaluating food safety is crucial. Herein, a ratiometric test strip was proposed by using green-yellow fluorescent d-penicillamine capped silver nanocluster (DPA-AgNCs) as indicator and red-emitting bimetallic gold/silver nanoclusters (AuAgNCs) as an internal reference, providing a real-time and visual monitoring system for food freshness. Results showed that the as-prepared DPA-AgNCs displayed an excellent response and good sensitivity for volatile basic nitrogens (VBNs), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.51 μM and 0.08 ppm for spermidine and ammonia hydroxide, respectively. Subsequently, a ratiometric test strip was developed to visually monitor ammonia vapour, displaying an obvious fluorescence colour variation from mustard to deep-red. Moreover, the presented ratiometric test strip was successfully applied for non-contact and visual evaluating and monitoring VBNs in the shrimp sample, showing high potential for in-situ monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescent test strip
VBNs
Food freshness

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of loaded DPA-AgNCs/AuAgNCs ratiometric fluorescent test strip
VBNs response of the ratiometric test strip
Monitoring of shrimp samples using the ratiometric test strips
Optimization of fabrication conditions of the strips
Visual monitoring of VBNs in seafood freshness

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of the ratiometric test strip and application for real-time and visual monitoring of VBNs and evaluation of seafood freshness.

(A) The UV–vis absorption (orange line) and fluorescence excitation and emission (blue line) spectra of DPA-AgNCs. (B) The fluorescence intensity of DPA-AgNCs with different volume ratios (i.e., fe = 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, and 100%), (Inset: the fluorescence picture of DPA-AgNCs with different volume ratio). (C) The PL lifetime spectra of DPA-AgNCs. (D) The IR spectra of DPA (blue line) and DPA-AgNCs (orange line). (E) XPS survey of DPA-AgNCs. (F) Ag 3d XPS spectrum of DPA-AgNCs. (G) TEM and TEM-EDX elemental mapping of DPA-AgNCs. (H) The UV–vis absorption (orange line) and fluorescence excitation and emission (blue line) spectra of AuAgNCs. (I) The fluorescence intensity of AuAgNCs with different volume ratios (i.e., fwater = 0%, 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, and 80%), (Inset: the fluorescence picture of AuAgNCs with different volume ratio). (J) TEM and TEM-EDX elemental mapping of AuAgNCs.

(A) (a) The fluorescence response of DPA-AgNCs to seven different VBNs (500 μM of histamine, putrescine, spermidine, cadaverine, tyramine, and spermine, as well as 50 ppm of NH3), including (left) the recorded fluorescence spectra and (right) the peak PL intensity. (b – c) The fluorescence response of DPA-AgNCs to different concentrations of (b) spermidine (1, 5, 10, 20, 40 and 50 μM) and (c) NH3 (0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 25, 40, and 50 ppm), including (left) the recorded fluorescence spectra and (right) the calibration curve acquired from F0-F as a function of concentrations. F0 and F were respectively the PL intensities at 550 nm of DPA-AgNCs in the absence and presence of the targets. (B) The SEM images of cotton paper, AuAgNCs, DPA-AgNCs, and ratiometric test strip under different magnifications: (Ⅰ) 100 × (scale bar 100 μm); (Ⅱ) 300 × (scale bar 20 μm); (III) 2.0 k (scale bar 2 μm). (C) The fluorescence pictures of (a) loaded DPA-AgNCs test strip and (b) ratiometric test strip (Ⅰ) before (blank) after being treated with different vapours (H2O, 99.5% MeOH, 99.5% EtOAc, 99.9% IPA, EtOH, 50 μg/mLH2S, and 1000 ppm NH3); (Ⅱ) after being treated with ammonia vapour at 1000 ppm every 4 min for 15 min; (III) after being treated with ammonia vapour at different concentrations (0, 100, 200, 300, 500, 600, 800, and 1000 ppm) for 15 min.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of DPA-AgNCs after adding spermidine (0, 80, and 120 μ). (B) IR spectra of DPA-AgNCs with or without NH3. (C) XPS survey of DPA-AgNCs after adding ammonia hydroxide. (D) Ag 3d XPS spectrum of DPA-AgNCs after adding NH3. (E) TEM image of DPA-AgNCs after adding NH3. (F) DLS of DPA-AgNCs after adding NH3 (0, 20, and 40 ppm). (G) SEM of the ratiometric test strip after adding NH3. (H) PL lifetime of DPA-AgNCs before and after adding NH3.

The optimization of the volume ratio of DPA-AgNCs and AuAgNCs for fabricated ratiometric test strips, and monitoring the shrimp freshness using these test strips (A: VDPA-AgNCs: VAuAgNCs = 3.9: 0.1, B: VDPA-AgNCs: VAuAgNCs = 3.8: 0.2, C: VDPA-AgNCs: VAuAgNCs = 3.7: 0.3, D: VDPA-AgNCs: VAuAgNCs = 3.6: 0.4).

Freshness monitoring of shrimp using the designed ratiometric test strip at (A) 28 °C and (B) 4 °C, with (Ⅰ) the photographs of the ratiometric test strip in the presence of shrimp under UV light, and (Ⅱ) the change of total VBNs contents in shrimp and G/R value (P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Dual-signal fluorescent test strips for spoilage sensing of packaged seafood: Visual monitoring of volatile basic nitrogens

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the food safety issues abounding, exploring reliable and efficient methods for evaluating food safety is crucial. Herein, a ratiometric test strip was proposed by using green-yellow fluorescent d-penicillamine capped silver nanocluster (DPA-AgNCs) as indicator and red-emitting bimetallic gold/silver nanoclusters (AuAgNCs) as an internal reference, providing a real-time and visual monitoring system for food freshness. Results showed that the as-prepared DPA-AgNCs displayed an excellent response and good sensitivity for volatile basic nitrogens (VBNs), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.51 μM and 0.08 ppm for spermidine and ammonia hydroxide, respectively. Subsequently, a ratiometric test strip was developed to visually monitor ammonia vapour, displaying an obvious fluorescence colour variation from mustard to deep-red. Moreover, the presented ratiometric test strip was successfully applied for non-contact and visual evaluating and monitoring VBNs in the shrimp sample, showing high potential for in-situ monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Food spoilage is a crucial food safety issue that threatens human health and restricts economic development (He et al., 2022; Zeng et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020a; Zhu et al., 2021). Exploring reliable and effective strategies for real-time monitoring of food freshness is of great significance. The total volatile basic nitrogens (total VBNs) including ammonia and biogenic amines, produced through the action of microorganisms during food storage, are considered important markers for monitoring food freshness (Johnson Esua et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022c). Conventional analytical methods for detecting VBNs mainly include electronic nose or tongue (Andre et al., 2022; Esua et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2022), instrumental techniques (He et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022a), and capillary electrophoresis (Cortacero-Ramírez et al., 2007; Sun et al., 2021), although with high accuracy, the operation of these methods is time-consuming, complex, and destructive, which cannot meet the demands of in-situ and visual recognition of food freshness throughout the supply chain (Esua et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2019a; Huang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022a).

In the scene of rapid, non-destructive, and visual detection, fluorescence-based optical sensors are highly promising methods due to their portability, simplicity, and sensitivity (Guo et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019). For this reason, several pH-sensitive fluorophores, including fluorescein isothiocyanate (Jia et al., 2019; Quan et al., 2021), 2-methoxy-2,4-diphenyl-3(2H)furanone (Jeon et al., 2020), chloro-nitrobenzoox- adiazoles (Yamaguchi et al., 2014), and carbon dots (Zhang et al., 2020b), have been designed and used to monitor VBNs. However, most organic fluorophores-based fluorescence sensors perform relatively poorly owing to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), especially in solid-state luminescence applications. By comparison, the occurrence of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) has provided a new pathway for the fabrication of fluorescence sensing systems because it allows AIE fluorogens (AIEgens) to work in solid-state systems (Chen et al., 2022). Recently, several AIEgens with VBNs-responsive characteristics, such as 4-chloro-2-(2-hydroxy-5-(methoxycarbonyl)-phenylamino)-benzoic acid (Zhang et al., 2019), 4-(dimethylamino)styryl)uinoxaline-2(1H)-one (Chen et al., 2022), poly(2-amino-4H-1,3-oxazine)s (Hu et al., 2019), have been proposed and applied for detection of VBNs and monitoring of food freshness. Although these strategies provide reliable probes for VBNs, they require toxic precursors and tedious synthesis processes, thus restricting their applications in food quality and safety evaluation.

Compared with organic AIEgens, metal nanoclusters (MNCs) with AIE properties are a novel class of luminophores with ultrasmall (<10 nm) size, gentle synthesis, excellent photostability, long emission lifetimes, large Stokes shift, fluorescence tunability, and good biocompatibility (Huang et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). These properties give MNCs the prospect of broader application in food quality and freshness evaluation. Our group proposed the use of the loaded copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) fluorescence tag for in-situ and real-time evaluation of salmon freshness (Zhang et al., 2022c). Similarly, Han et al. (2018) reported a CuNCs-based histamine-responsive fluorescent tag with AIE properties for visual monitoring of histamine in foodstuffs. Although accurate VBNs-responsive probes can be acquired from these approaches, these sensing systems for MNCs depend on the single-signal response (on/off model) and perform poorly due to the interference factors from the test environments (Chen et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2022). In contrast, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor can overcome these obstacles and obtain more accurate results by self-calibrating the fluorescent intensities of two signal units. Ni et al. (2020) developed an amine-responsive sensor by simultaneously encapsulating green/red-emitting Ru/Pt complex cations into a metal–organic framework and loaded it onto test strips to monitor different VBNs vapours. Our group proposed a ratiometric fluorescent tag using silver nanoclusters (NAC-AgNCs) capped with N-acetyl-l-cysteine and gold nanoclusters capped glutathione (GSH-AuNCs) as an indicator and internal reference, respectively, and displayed that this tag exhibited an obvious fluorescent colour change when monitoring seafood freshness (Zhang et al., 2022b). Jiang et al. (2022) designed a ratiometric fluorescent probe (PTNC) and loaded it onto filter paper to fabricate test strips and observed a significant change in fluorescence from orange to green upon contact with cadaverine. In the library of these presented ratiometric fluorescent tags, only a few are based on MNCs with AIE properties. Therefore, the development of a portable, visual, and ultrasensitive ratiometric sensing strategy based on MNCs to monitor seafood freshness is promising and practical in the food industry.

Herein, a portable ratiometric fluorescent test strip based on MNCs with AIE properties was developed for visual recognition of VBNs and evaluation of seafood freshness, as depicted in Fig. 1 . The ratiometric fluorescence sensor was designed by using green-yellow emitting d-penicillamine capped silver nanoclusters (DPA-AgNCs) as indicators and red emitting bimetallic gold/silver nanoclusters (AuAgNCs) as the internal reference. Following the physical deposition of the presented ratiometric fluorescence probe on cotton paper, the ratiometric test strips were successfully prepared and applied to detect VBNs vapour by ratiometric fluorescence reaction, as well as ammonia hydroxide as a model target. For actual sample detection, the presented test strip was employed to visually monitor shrimp at different temperatures (4 and 28 °C), exhibiting a highly identifiable fluorescence colour change from mustard to yellow–brown to deep-red. Therefore, the fabricated ratiometric test strips offer a promising candidate for real-time assessment of seafood freshness.

Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.9%), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), d-penicillamine (DPA, 98.0%), 1-adamantane mercaptan (1-AD), tetraoctylammonium bromide (TOAB), methanol (MeOH, 99.5%), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2, 99.8%), ethyl acetate (EtOAc, 99.5%), isopropanol (IPA), hydrogen sulfide (H2S, 100.0 μg/mL), spermine (99%), Bis(diphenylphosphino)methane (DMMP), histamine (97.0%), putrescine (98.5%), spermidine (99.0%), cadaverine (96.5%), and ammonium hydroxide (NH3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9%+) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 98%) were bought from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals are of analytical grade.

Fluorescence spectra for DPA-AgNCs and AuAgNCs were collected on a Spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, Edinburgh FS5, Livingston, UK) with a slit width of 5 nm. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra for DPA-AgNCs and AuAgNCs were measured on a U-3900H spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) from 200 to 600 nm. Infrared (IR) characterizations were investigated by a Nicolet Continuum Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected by a Talos F200X system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained by an Escalab Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, USA). The morphologies of the fabricated test strip were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi Regulus 8100, Hitachi Co. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential of DPA-AgNCs with or without VBNs were conducted on a Zetasizer from Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK). The absolute quantum yield (QY) was measured by an absolute PL QY spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Nanolog FL3-2 IHR, Pairs, France).

The DPA-AgNCs were prepared following a previous study with slight modifications (Jia et al., 2014). Typically, 4 mL of 0.202 M freshly prepared DPA solution and 1.2 mL of 0.1 M AgNO3 solution were added to 10 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the mixture was heated in a commercial microwave oven (600 W, Midea Group Co., Ltd., Foshan, China) for 200 s. To remove the excess ligands, the resulting product was collected by EtOH and centrifugated at 6603 g for 8 min, and the resultant precipitates were dispersed in 20 mL of EtOH (6 mM, Ag atom basis), which was stored in a refrigerator (Midea Group Co., Ltd., Foshan, China) at 4 °C for further uses.

The synthesis of AuAgNCs was slightly adapted from a reported procedure (Jin et al., 2018). Specifically, 40 mg of HAuCl4·3H2O and 60 mg of AgNO3 were dissolved in 2 mL of MeOH and 30 mL CH2Cl2 mixture solution containing 100 mg of TOAB with magnetic stirring for 10 min. The solution turned into an orange suspension. Later, 50 mg of DPPM and 50 mg of 1-AD were added to the mixture and the colour of the obtained solution gradually changed to colourless. After stirring for 30 min, 4 mL of 10 mg/mL freshly prepared NaBH4 solution was added. Then, the colour immediately turned black. The crude product was acquired by rotary evaporation after reaction for 5 h. Finally, 30 mL of MeOH was added and the resulting solution turned red after stewing for 12 h, showing the product was pure AuAgNCs.

Different concentrations (4, 20, 40, 80, and 200 μM) of biogenic amines (histamine, putrescine, spermidine, cadaverine, tyramine, and spermine) standard solutions (500 μL) were mixed with 1500 μL of 3 mM DPA-AgNCs for 5 min. Then, 500 μL of the mixture was then transferred to 700 μL of quartz cuvette for fluorescence measurements.

NH3 (500 μL) with known concentrations (0.4, 2, 4, 20, 40, 100, 160, and 200 ppm) was mixed with 1500 μL of 3 mM DPA-AgNCs for 5 min. Then, 500 μL of the mixture was transferred to 700 μL of quartz cuvette for fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of loaded DPA-AgNCs/AuAgNCs ratiometric fluorescent test strip
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram of the fabrication of loaded DPA-AgNCs/AuAgNCs ratiometric fluorescent test strip (ratiometric test strip for short) is revealed in Fig. 1. Typically, 0.4 mL of AuAgNCs (0.088 mM, Au atom basis) and 3.6 mL of DPA-AgNCs (6 mM, Ag atom basis) were added into a 5 mL tube to prepare the ratiometric probe. Later, a volume of 100 μL of the fabricated ratiometric probe was drop-casted onto the surface of circle fibre cotton paper (1 cm). After drying in a drying oven (DHG-9023A, Shanghai Jiecheng Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) at 60 °C for 2 h, the ratiometric test strip was obtained. The loaded DPA-AgNCs test strip and loaded AuAgNCs test strip were acquired in a similar step except that ethanol solution was used instead of AuAgNCs (0.4 mL) and DPA-AgNCs (3.6 mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: VBNs response of the ratiometric test strip
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the as-prepared ratiometric test strip was explored before exploring the response behaviours of the ratiometric test strip to VBNs. Three millilitres of solutions of H2S (100 μg/mL), EtOH, IPA, GSH, EtOAc, MeOH, NH3 (1000 ppm), Cys and water were added to the bottom of 35 mm of a plastic culture dish, and the ratiometric test strips were placed on the top of the culture dish. After being exposed for 20 min, the fluorescent images of the corresponding test strips were acquired by smartphone (Xiaomi Co., Beijing, China) under UV light.

The response behaviours of the fabricated ratiometric test strips variations to a series of NH3 solutions (i.e., 100, 200, 300, 500, 600, 800, and 1000 ppm) were studied. The fluorescent variations of test strips were recorded by smartphone under UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of shrimp samples using the ratiometric test strips
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh greasy-back shrimp was purchased from 7Fresh (Guangzhou, China). As displayed in Fig. 1, 45 ± 0.45 g of shrimps were placed on the inner bottom of the culture dish (150 mm), while the ratiometric test strip was stuck on the inner top of the culture dish. The samples were stored at 4 and 28 °C to explore the dynamic freshness variations, and fluorescent colour changes of the test strips were recorded at regular intervals by a smartphone. For comparison, the total VBNs value in shrimp samples was detected by the Kjeldahl method (EU Commission Regulation), and the rejection limit of the total VBNs is 30 mg/100 g.

All the measurements were performed in triplicate and results were expressed as means ± standard deviations. Figures were plotted and appropriate statistical analyses were concluded by Prism 9 (GraphPad Software, California, USA).

The optical properties of the as-prepared DPA-AgNCs were explored by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopies. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of DPA-AgNCs (orange line in Fig. 2 A) exhibited a weak peak at 304 nm, attributing to the π–π* transition of the ligands. When excited at 295 nm, the DPA-AgNCs solution exhibited green-yellow luminescence with a maximum wavelength at 550 nm. The AIE properties of the DPA-AgNCs were confirmed by monitoring the change in fluorescence intensity in EtOH/water mixtures with variable water volume fractions. As revealed in Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity of the DPA-AgNCs decreased significantly with increasing the volume fraction of water from 10% to 90%, while the maximum emission peaks ranged from 550 nm to 600 nm. The results proved that DPA-AgNCs were typical AIEgens (Zhang et al., 2022c). In addition, the long photoluminescence (PL) lifetime was an important characteristic of MNCs with AIE properties. Fig. 2C exhibits the PL lifetime spectrum of the as-prepared DPA-AgNCs. There was a long PL lifetime of 22.32 μs, indicating that DPA-AgNCs possessed high emission efficiency. The quantum yield (QY) of DPA-AgNCs was measured to be 0.42, which was higher than the QY of most reported MNCs (Cai et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2013; Shu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2022c). The long PL lifetime and 255 nm of Stokes shift indicated that the PL emission of the DPA-AgNCs could be attributed to the ligand-to-metal charge transfer from the S atom of DPA to the Ag core (Jia et al., 2014).

IR spectroscopy was applied to evaluate the binding of DPA on the surface of DPA-AgNCs. As revealed in Fig. 2D, DPA exhibited the typical -SH stretching vibration peak at 2524.5 cm−1 (dark-blue line). Compared with DPA, the peak at 2524.5 cm−1 disappeared after DPA-AgNCs (orange line) formed, confirming the formation of Ag – S bonding between DPA and AgNCs. The structural characterizations of DPA-AgNCs were also explored by XPS and Fig. 2E exhibited the XPS full spectrum of the DPA-AgNCs, which indicated that the as-prepared DPA-AgNCs were composed of O (38.98%), N (7.18%), Ag (5.30%), C (41.39%), and S (7.14%) elements. Meanwhile, the Ag 3d spectrum of DPA-AgNCs (Fig. 2F) showed two characteristic peaks at 374.2 eV and 368.2 eV, referring to the binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively. Besides, the morphology and elemental composition of the synthesized DPA-AgNCs were characterized by TEM and TEM-EDX elemental analysis. Fig. 2G depicts that the synthesized DPA-AgNCs exhibited a compact aggregate state composed of small particles with an average hydrated diameter of 2.58 ± 0.64 nm. Meanwhile, Ag, N, C, O, and S were uniformly distributed in DPA-AgNCs, which was consistent with the XPS results. Thus, all results indicated that the DPA-AgNCs were successfully prepared.

Then, the optical properties, morphology and elemental composition of AuAgNCs were explored. As depicted in Fig. 2H, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the synthesized AuAgNCs exhibited two peaks centred at 470 and 545 nm (orange line in Fig. 2H). The fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared AuAgNCs revealed that there was an emission peak at 695 nm with a maximum excitation wavelength of 297 nm. The AIE properties of the synthesized AuAgNCs were explored by monitoring their fluorescence intensity variations in MeOH/water mixtures with variable water volume fractions. As revealed in Fig. 2I, the fluorescence intensity of the AuAgNCs was enhanced significantly as the volume fraction of water was increased from 10% to 80%, which confirmed that AuAgNCs were typical AIEgens. The morphology and elemental composition of the prepared AuAgNCs were characterized by TEM. As revealed in Fig. 2J, the AuAgNCs showed spherical clusters with an average particle diameter of 260 nm. The TEM-EDX elemental mapping results showed that the elements Au, Ag, P, S, and O were uniformly distributed in the synthesized AuAgNCs. All these results demonstrated the successful preparation of AuAgNCs.

The ability of the as-prepared DPA-AgNCs for quantitative detection of VBNs was studied. As shown in Fig. 3 A (a), the fluorescence response of DPA-AgNCs to different types of VBNs (histamine, putrescine, spermidine, cadaverine, tyramine, spermine, as well as NH3) was measured, and water was employed as control. The presented DPA-AgNCs displayed different fluorescence response abilities to different VBNs, specifically to putrescine, spermidine, as well as NH3. Then, the response behaviours of DPA-AgNCs toward spermidine and NH3 were also investigated. Fig. 3A (b) displayed the fluorescence response of the DPA-AgNCs to a series of spermidine (1, 5, 10, 20, 40 and 50 μM). It can be found from Fig. 3A (b) that the F0-F (F0 and F were the PL intensities at 550 nm of DPA-AgNCs in the absence and presence of the targets, respectively.) has an excellent linear relationship against spermidine concentrations from 1 to 50 μM (R2 = 0.9956), and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.51 μM (S/N = 3), which was better than reported works (Yan et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022b). For NH3 (Fig. 3A (c)), the plot of F0-F versus the concentration of NH3 showed a piecewise linear relationship with two linear ranges of NH3 from 0.1 to 10 ppm (R2 = 0.9914) and 10 to 50 ppm (R2 = 0.9969), respectively, with the LOD of 0.08 ppm (S/N = 3). In addition, the fabricated sensor was also compared with other reported VBNs detection methods based on fluorescence nanomaterials (Table S1). This work displayed a comparable or better LOD than most previous studies. Thus, the fabricated DPA-AgNCs were highly sensitive in detecting spermidine and NH3, confirming their good potential for monitoring of VBNs.

After investigating the sensing performance of the prepared DPA-AgNCs, the test strips were prepared and their fluorescence performances were explored. As revealed in Fig. 3B, there was no fluorescence emission for cotton paper, while the loaded AuAgNCs test strip and loaded DPA-AgNCs test strips showed red and green-yellow fluorescence, respectively. Mustard fluorescence was also observed for the fabricated ratiometric test strip, indicating the successful fabrication of the ratiometric test strip. Besides, the SEM images were applied to visually observe the morphology of the fabricated test strips. As revealed in Fig. 3B, the cotton paper showed a loose and porous structure consisting of fibres with a rough and uneven surface (Blank). After the deposition of AuAgNCs, many larger spherical particles appeared on the surface of fibres, manifesting the formation of larger aggregates on the surface of fibres. Similarly, there were lots of flaky particles on the surfaces of the DPA-AgNCs modified cotton paper, indicating the formation of larger DPA-AgNCs aggregates on the cotton paper surface after the volatilization of EtOH. For the cotton paper co-deposited with AuAgNCs and DPA-AgNCs, more particles were mixed and deposited on the surface of fibres, confirming the successful fabrication of the ratiometric test strip.

Following the successful fabrication of the loaded DPA-AgNCs test strip, the loaded AuAgNCs test strip, and the ratiometric test strip, the fluorescence response of these test strips was studied using ammonia as a model analyte. First, the selectivity of the loaded DPA-AgNCs test strip was explored using nine different volatile compounds (i.e., water, MeOH, EtOAc, GSH, IPA, EtOH, H2S, NH3, and Cys) generated during seafood spoilage as model analytes. As depicted in Fig. 3C (a-I), the loaded DPA-AgNCs test strip emitted green-yellow fluorescence under UV light. After 15 min of treatment, no perceptible variations in fluorescence intensity were observed after being treated with water, MeOH, EtOAc, GSH, IPA, EtOH, H2S, and Cys, while the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly after exposure to NH3, proving the good selectivity of the loaded DPA-AgNCs test strip.

Next, the sensitivity of the loaded DPA-AgNCs test strip for monitoring of VBNs vapour was investigated in terms of (1) exposure to 1000 ppm of NH3 every 4 min for 15 min (Fig. 3C (a- II)) and (2) exposure to NH3 with different concentrations (0–1000 ppm) for 15 min (Fig. 3C (a-III)). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased by 1000 ppm of NH3 with the treatment time increased, and the test strips were gradually quenched as the NH3 concentration increased from 0 to 1000 ppm in a certain treatment time (15 min). Besides, the sensitivity of the loaded AuAgNCs test strip for detecting ammonia vapour was studied, and results are shown in Fig. S1, indicating that the loaded AuAgNCs test strip was insensitive to VBNs.

In addition, the fluorescence response of the ratiometric test strip was also explored. Fig. 3C (b-I) showed the selectivity results of the test strip for nine different volatile compounds, confirming the good selectivity for the detection of VBNs. As depicted in Fig. 3C (b-II), the fluorescence colour of the presented test strip was mustard. As the storage time increased, a distinct variation from mustard to deep-red was clearly observed. Meanwhile, after 20 min of treatment, the fluorescence colour of the fabricated ratiometric test strip varied from mustard to chrome yellow to deep-red as the NH3 concentration increased from 0 to 1000 ppm (Fig. 3C (b-III)). These results indicated that the presented ratiometric test strip displayed a distinct fluorescence response to NH3. Based on its portability and sensitivity, the fabricated test strip had good potential for visual recognition of seafood freshness.

As the changes in fluorescence of the ratiometric test strip were due to the variations in fluorescence intensity of DPA-AgNCs in response to the VBNs, the structural variations of DPA-AgNCs could reflect the structural variations of the ratiometric probe during the VBNs monitoring. The sensing mechanism of the DPA-AgNCs toward VBNs was analyzed using UV–vis, IR, zeta potential, XPS, TEM, DLS, SEM, as well as PL lifetime, and NH3 was applied as a model analyte. As revealed in Fig. 4 A, DPA-AgNCs displayed a UV–vis absorption peak at 304 nm, which disappeared after adding NH3, demonstrating that the fluorescence quenching of DPA-AgNCs might be attributed to the dynamic quenching mechanism. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the peak intensity at 1612 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1, assigned to –NH bending and stretching vibration, was enhanced after the addition of NH3. The XPS measurements (Fig. 4C) showed an increase in N element (10.82%) after adding the NH3, which was consistent with the results from IR, manifesting that the NH3 was bonded on the surface of DPA-AgNCs. The Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 4D) result showed that the formation of bonding between DPA-AgNCs and NH3 could not change the oxidation state of the Ag core. In the presence of NH3, the compact and ordered aggregates structure was disrupted, and spherical particles were observed by TEM (Fig. 4E). As revealed in Fig. 4F, the average hydrodynamic diameter of DPA-AgNCs decreased as the concentration of NH3 increased. Fig. 4G depicts the SEM images of the ratiometric test strip after being exposed to shrimp for 10 h. Compared with the ratiometric test strip without any treatments (Fig. 3B), the diameters of aggregates became smaller after adding NH3, which was consistent with the results obtained from DLS and TEM. Most importantly, the obtained PL lifetime value of the DPA-AgNCs was shortened after adding NH3, manifesting that the PL of DPA-AgNCs was affected by VBNs through dynamic quenching. Thus, the above results proved that the sensing mechanism in this study was due to the dynamic quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of fabrication conditions of the strips
Visual monitoring of VBNs in seafood freshness
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the above results, the fabricated ratiometric test strip was applied to real-time monitor the VBNs in shrimp and evaluate their freshness at different storage temperatures (28 (Fig. 6A) and 4 °C (Fig. 6B)). The total VBNs contents in shrimps stored at different times were measured to estimate the accuracy of the developed ratiometric test strips. In addition, the fluorescence response of the fabricated ratiometric test strip could be quantitated by G/R value (Fig. 6 (Ⅱ)).

As illustrated in Fig. 6A, the total content of VBNs in fresh shrimp was 6.89 mg/100 g, increasing to 14.09 mg/100 g after 3 h. Meanwhile, the G/R value decreased from 0.99 to 0.95, and the corresponding colour of the ratiometric test strips was yellow–brown, indicating the secondary fresh status. After 5, 7, and 9 h of storage, the total contents of VBNs increased to 21.32, 26.04 and 30.52 mg/100 g, respectively, as well as the fluorescence of the ratiometric test strips varied to chrome yellow, turning to red, and deep-red, and the corresponding G/R value was in the range of 0.92–0.86, 0.86–0.70, and <0.70, reflecting the different freshness status of decomposed, slight spoiled, and completed spoiled, respectively.

Similar phenomena were observed under storage conditions at 4 °C, as revealed in Fig. 6B. The initial total VBNs value was 6.88 mg/100 g and the G/R value was more than 0.96, as well as the fluorescence colour of the test strips was mustard. After 2, 3 and 4 d of storage, the values increased to 18.41, 25.82, and 30.06 mg/100 g, and the G/R value was in the range of 0.96–0.92, 0.86–0.70, and <0.70, respectively. At these time points, the fluorescence colour of the test strips varied to yellow–brown, turning to red, and deep-red, respectively, indicating a variation in freshness status to second fresh, slightly spoiled, and completed spoiled, respectively.

To further validate the accuracy of the prepared test strip, the total viable counts (TVC) in shrimps were measured for comparison. According to the International Committee on Microbial Specifications (ICMSF), the safe limit of TVC content in seafood is 107 CFU/g (ICMSF, 1996). Fig. S2 showed the dynamic change process of TVC versus storage periods. For storage at 28 °C (Figure S2A), the average value of TVC increased from 1.4 × 104 CFU/g to 1.5 × 105 CFU/g after 3 h. When the shrimp was stored for 9 h, the TVC content rose to 2.5 × 107 CFU/g, proving the complete spoilage of the shrimp. For storage at 4 °C, the TVC content in fresh shrimp was 1.8 × 104 CFU/g, increasing to 1.9 × 107 CFU/g after 4 d (Fig. S2B), indicating that the shrimp was completely spoiled. This result was in line with those obtained from total VBNs in shrimp and colour changes of the test strip, which confirmed that the fabricated test strip has good accuracy for monitoring seafood freshness.

Besides, the fabricated ratiometric test strips were compared with several reported methods. As shown in Table S1, the fabricated DPA-AgNCs possessed excellent QY (42%), which was higher than most of those reported fluorescence nanomaterials. As discussed previously, most organic fluorophores-based fluorescence sensors perform relatively poorly owing to the ACQ, which limited their application in food freshness monitoring (Li et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2022). In addition, some sensors depend on the single-signal response and perform poorly due to the interference factors from the test environments (Li et al., 2020). In this study, the fabricated ratiometric test strips were prepared based on MNCs with AIE properties and worked in solid form. Compared with a single-signal sensor, these test strips exhibited a distinguishing fluorescent colour variation during monitoring seafood freshness. Collectively, the above results proved that the fabricated ratiometric test strips could be applied as a gas sensing platform for non-destructive and real-time monitoring of packaging seafood freshness conveniently.

A ratiometric test strip was prepared for in-situ, non-contact, and visual detection of VBNs as well as evaluation of seafood freshness, with DPA-AgNCs and AuAgNCs serving as the indicator and internal reference, respectively. Owing to its great fluorescence property, the DPA-AgNCs exhibited a rapid fluorescence response toward VBNs based on the dynamic quenching mechanism, with the LOD of 0.51 μM and 0.08 ppm for spermidine as well as NH3, respectively. Subsequently, a ratiometric test strip was prepared and applied to detect ammonia vapour, exhibiting a distinct fluorescence colour variation from mustard to deep-red. To further explore its actual application potential, the proposed ratiometric test strip was used to evaluate the freshness of shrimp and its results were consistent with the changes in total VBNs values. Results from this study suggested that the fabricated ratiometric test strip should act as a promising VBNs sensing system to evaluate and monitor food freshness with prospects for actual applications in the food industry.
Wenyang Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Investigation. Ji Ma: Validation. Da-Wen Sun: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021A1515110396) for its support. This research was also supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (3217161084), the Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Intelligent Sensing and Quality Control of Agricultural Products (202102100009), Laboratory of Lingnan Modern Agriculture Project (NZ2021035), the Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Plan Projects (2020A1414010160), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515012489, 2020A1515010936), the Contemporary International Collaborative Research Centre of Guangdong Province on Food Innovative Processing and Intelligent Control (2019A050519001) and the Common Technical Innovation Team of Guangdong Province on Preservation and Logistics of Agricultural Products (2022KJ101).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135725.

ce:section.ce:para:
